title: Adhesive-bound sketchbook
subtitle: A flex-spine glued sketchbook without any sewing
excerpt: An adhesive-bound sketchbook uses a flexible PVA spine to hold loose pages together in a sturdy block. No sewing is required, and the finished book opens flat. It is a practical first binding project and produces a book that handles well for drawing and watercolour.
sourceNotes: Adhesive-bound binding is a modern technique widely documented in bookbinding instruction manuals of the mid-to-late 20th century. The flex-spine method described here is consistent with the double-fan adhesive approach.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flexible adhesive spine that allows a book to open flat without cracking the glue line, achieved by using undiluted PVA in multiple thin layers.
A strip of open-weave cloth (mull) or kraft paper glued across the spine of a text block to bridge the gaps between pages and reinforce the adhesive layer.

content.text: An adhesive-bound sketchbook suits cartridge paper, watercolour paper, and mixed-media paper in a range of weights. The binding method works by fanning the pages in alternating directions and applying PVA in multiple thin passes until the adhesive penetrates the paper edges. A strip of book mull covers the spine and provides the structural reinforcement a sewn binding gets from the thread. The finished book opens fully flat, which is what a sketchbook needs.

attrs.heading: Bookbinding adhesives
attrs.body: PVA is safe for indoor use without special ventilation. Use acid-free PVA for any sketchbook you want to last. Allow each adhesive layer to dry fully before applying the next.

items.name: Drawing or mixed-media paper, 150 to 200 gsm
items.notes: Cut to the finished page size. Grain direction parallel to the spine (long grain) prevents cockling.

items.name: PVA, acid-free
items.notes: Used undiluted for the spine layers.

items.name: Book mull (super)
items.notes: Cut to the spine height with 15 mm wings on each side for attaching to the cover boards.

items.name: Bookbinding board, 1.5 mm
items.notes: For the front and back covers, cut 3 mm larger than the page on all three outer edges.

items.name: Paste brush, 50 mm flat
items.notes: Dedicated to PVA; clean immediately after use with warm water.

items.name: Bulldog clips (assorted)
items.notes: For clamping the page block while the adhesive dries.

content.text: Stack the pages in order with the spine edges perfectly aligned. Place two pressing boards on the outer faces and clamp firmly with binder clips. The spine edge should project 5 mm beyond the boards so you can work the adhesive into it freely. Place the clamped block in a vice or wedge it between heavy objects so the spine faces up and is held rigidly.

Fan the pages toward you slightly and brush undiluted PVA along the spine, working it into the gaps between pages. Tap the block upright to bring all pages back to vertical alignment. Fan the pages the other way and apply another coat from the opposite angle. This double-fan method ensures PVA reaches between every page edge. Allow to dry completely, around 30 minutes.
Apply a second pass of PVA to the spine surface. While it is still wet, lay the book mull

centrally across the spine, with the wings folded down flat against the pressing boards. Press with a bone folder to bed the mull into the adhesive. Apply a final thin coat of PVA over the mull. Allow to dry fully before unclamping, at least one hour.

Cut the cover boards 3 mm larger than the pages at the head, tail, and foredge. Apply PVA to the mull wing on one side and press the corresponding board onto it, keeping an equal square on all three outer edges. Repeat for the other board. Once both boards are in place, apply a thin coat of PVA across the spine over the mull and press a strip of decorative paper or cover material across it to protect and neaten the exposed spine. Allow to dry under weight overnight.

becomes fully flexible once the PVA has cured, which takes 24 hours in a warm room. The book should open flat to any page without cracking. If a page pulls free, work a little fresh PVA into the gap with a fine brush and clamp the book shut while it dries.

items.cause: Insufficient adhesive penetration on the first pass.
items.fix: Apply more PVA passes before the mull stage. Three or four thin passes are more effective than one heavy coat.

items.symptom: The book will not open flat
items.cause: The PVA dried too thick across the spine, forming a rigid bar rather than a flexible joint.
items.fix: Use undiluted PVA in thin coats only. If the spine is already rigid, flex the book gently and repeatedly; PVA becomes slightly more flexible once fully cured.

items.symptom: The cover boards are cockled after drying
items.cause: The PVA on the mull wings pulled the boards inward as it dried.
items.fix: Place the book under heavy boards overnight. For future bindings, dampen the inside face of each cover board lightly before attaching, then press; the board dries flat.
